coaching.coaching: Plan how per-term coverage verification for AC1 proof will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: per-term coverage verification for AC1 proof

coaching.coaching: Plan how AC2 gate-interaction proof command coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: AC2 gate-interaction proof command coverage

coaching.coaching: Pin down session_log type semantics to be migrated with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: session_log type semantics to be migrated

coaching.coaching: Consider a short spike to de-risk remaining CLAUDE.md-deferral pointers across docs/ before committing the plan.
coaching.subject: remaining CLAUDE.md-deferral pointers across docs/

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the AC1 rg proving command with four separate existence checks, one per topic, e.g. `rg -q 'idea' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'parent_id' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'discovered_from' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md`.
evidence: The OR-pattern `idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags` matches any line containing ANY one of the four terms; `rg -c` counts matching lines; `-ge 4` only asserts ≥4 such lines exist, not that each term appears at least once.
Defect taxonomy #5: missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count of distinct topics.
findings.finding: AC1's rg proving command uses a single OR-pattern with -c and -ge 4, meaning four lines all containing only 'idea' (and none containing 'parent_id', 'discovered_from', or 'set-tags') would satisfy the count check — so the command does NOT verify that all four concept topics are present.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 proving command: `rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md` -ge 4
scenarios: An agent writes four paragraphs all about the 'idea' lifecycle and omits the parent_id/linking/tags sections; the rg -c count hits 4 and the AC passes despite three topics being absent.
findings.suggested_fix: rg -q 'idea' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'parent_id' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'discovered_from' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'event-schema' docs/ticket-model.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the AC2 proving command into three separate rg checks, one per named nuance, e.g. `rg -q 'plan-review gate' docs/concurrency.md && rg -q 'gate-exempt' docs/concurrency.md && rg -q -- '--force' docs/concurrency.md`.
evidence: The criterion states three distinct nuances must be added: (1) parent's own plan-review gate, (2) epics/stories not gate-exempt, (3) `--force` propagates. The OR-pattern passes on any single match.
Defect taxonomy #5: missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names multiple distinct items.
findings.finding: AC2's rg proving command uses a single OR-pattern `gate-exempt|plan-review gate|--force`, so a match on ANY ONE term exits 0 — an agent could add only `--force` to concurrency.md and the command would pass, leaving the other two stated nuances (parent's own plan-review gate; epics/stories not gate-exempt) unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 proving command: `rg -n 'gate-exempt|plan-review gate|--force' docs/concurrency.md`
scenarios: An agent appends a one-line note about `--force` propagation to I4a; `rg -n` finds that line and exits 0; the other two nuances are never added but the AC is considered done.
findings.suggested_fix: rg -q 'plan-review gate' docs/concurrency.md && rg -q 'gate-exempt' docs/concurrency.md && rg -q -- '--force' docs/concurrency.md

findings.finding: AC1's proving command uses `rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md` with a single alternation pattern; `rg -c` counts total lines matching ANY of the alternatives, not one per pattern. A document mentioning only `idea` four times would return count≥4 and pass, even if `parent_id`, `discovered_from`, and `set-tags` are completely absent — leaving three of the four required concept topics unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: rg -q 'idea' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'parent_id' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'discovered_from' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md && rg -q 'event-schema' docs/ticket-model.md

findings.finding: AC1's grep proof (`rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md` ≥ 4) is satisfiable by four hits of the single term `idea` (or any one term). It does not verify that all four named concept topics (idea lifecycle, hierarchy/containment via parent_id, linking via discovered_from, tags via set-tags) are each present as distinct documented topics — the AC would pass if three of the four topics are entirely absent but `idea` appears four times.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single `-c … -ge 4` check with five separate `rg -q` assertions (one per required term/topic) chained with `&&`, so each topic is independently gated.

findings.finding: AC1's shell proof (`rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md -ge 4`) counts lines matching ANY of the four terms, not that all four topics are substantively present. A file covering only 'idea' extensively (≥4 lines) with no mention of parent_id, discovered_from, or set-tags would still pass the grep while missing three of the four required topics.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — Proving shell command
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `-c` count check with four separate `rg -q` checks joined by `&&`, one per required topic term, so each topic must be individually present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required content for the new `## The session_log ticket type` section in docs/event-schema.md — enumerate the semantics that must be present (e.g., gate/lifecycle-exempt, excluded from graph/health compiles, excluded from default `list`, never Jira-synced, refuses STATUS events, permits non-blocking link relations) so an executor can write the section without reading CLAUDE.md.
evidence: event-schema.md line 46 (CREATE row): '**`session_log`** is a verbose-log type that is gate/lifecycle-exempt, excluded from the graph/health compiles (via `reduce_all_tickets(exclude_session_logs=…)`) and default `list`, never synced to Jira, and refuses `STATUS` (no claim/transition) — see CLAUDE.md "Session logs".'
Plan scope section: 'Out: editing `CLAUDE.md` (that is S2) and any code change. Docs-only.'

Plan What section: 'Replace the pointer in `docs/event-schema.md:46` (its CREATE row currently says *"see CLAUDE.md 'Session logs'"*) with a real `## The session_log ticket type` section carrying the type semantics.' — but no enumeration of those type semantics is given.
findings.finding: The plan instructs creation of a '## The session_log ticket type' section in docs/event-schema.md 'carrying the type semantics', but never enumerates what those semantics are. The sole current source is event-schema.md line 46's deferred pointer to 'CLAUDE.md Session logs' — and editing CLAUDE.md is explicitly out of scope. An executor writing this section must independently reconstruct the type semantics from CLAUDE.md without any specification of what must be covered.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC line 3: '`docs/event-schema.md` gains a `## The session_log ticket type` section AND no longer defers session_log semantics to CLAUDE.md.'
scenarios: An executor creates the new section but omits key behaviors (e.g., that session_logs refuse STATUS events, or are excluded from graph compiles) because the plan doesn't specify them.
Different executors produce different section content depending on their read of the unlisted CLAUDE.md, creating inconsistency.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, expand the session_log bullet to list the required semantic items: gate/lifecycle-exempt, excluded from `reduce_all_tickets` graph/health compiles and default `list`, never synced to Jira, refuses STATUS events (no claim/transition), permits `relates_to`/`discovered_from` but refuses `blocks`/`depends_on`, auto-rotation pointer to `.rebar/current_session_log`. Reference the CREATE row's in-line prose as the source of truth to copy from.

findings.finding: The plan says to 'promote the existing CREATE-row note into a real section' in `docs/event-schema.md` for `session_log`, but gives no instruction about the fate of the source CREATE-row entry. The CREATE row (event-schema.md line 46) is a dense, multi-sentence inline spec covering gate-exemption, lifecycle-exemption, graph exclusion, STATUS refusal, link restrictions, Jira exclusion, and the title convention. Without specifying whether the row is shortened, left as-is, or partially replaced by a pointer to the new section, an executor will either produce duplicate content (drift risk the plan explicitly wants to avoid) or silently strip the row's forward-compat/reducer context.
findings.location: What section / docs/event-schema.md — session_log section promotion

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the CREATE-row cell in event-schema.md should be updated to cross-reference the new `## The session_log ticket type` section (e.g., replace 'see CLAUDE.md "Session logs"' with 'see [§ The session_log ticket type](#the-session_log-ticket-type) below'), and add this to the AC3 proof.
evidence: Line 46 CREATE row ends with: 'see CLAUDE.md "Session logs"' — an inline cell reference, not just a standalone line

AC3 proving: `rg -q '## The session_log ticket type' docs/event-schema.md && [ "$(rg -c "see CLAUDE.md .Session logs" docs/event-schema.md)" = 0 ]` — only checks section existence and pointer removal, not whether the CREATE row cross-references the new section
Plan 'What' bullet 3 says 'Replace the pointer … with a real section' but does not explicitly address updating the CREATE-row cell's remaining text to point at the new section
findings.finding: The plan says to add a '## The session_log ticket type' section to event-schema.md and remove the 'see CLAUDE.md' pointer, but the CREATE-row cell (line 46) contains the CLAUDE.md reference inline within the event-types table. The AC only checks for the new section's existence and for absence of the pointer string — it does not require updating the CREATE-row prose itself to cross-reference the new section. After the work, the CREATE row may still carry orphaned or inconsistent inline text about session_log semantics (or remain silent on where to find them) even though the section now lives elsewhere in the same file.
findings.location: What / AC3 (docs/event-schema.md); event-schema.md line 46 CREATE row
scenarios: Implementer adds the new section and strips the 'see CLAUDE.md' phrase from the CREATE row, but the row then has no cross-reference to the new section — readers of the table cell get no navigation hint.
AC3 passes (section exists, pointer gone) but the CREATE row's inline prose is left silent or mismatched.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' bullet 3, add: 'Update the CREATE-row cell's inline session_log prose to replace the CLAUDE.md pointer with a same-file anchor link (e.g., `see [§ The session_log ticket type](#the-session_log-ticket-type)`).' Update AC3 to include: `rg -q '#the-session_log-ticket-type\|session_log ticket type' docs/event-schema.md` (or a similar anchor-check).

findings.finding: AC3 instructs removing the 'see CLAUDE.md Session logs' pointer from the CREATE row and adding a `## The session_log ticket type` section, but does not specify whether the CREATE row's existing ~1.5-line inline session_log behavioral description should be trimmed. Left as-is alongside the new dedicated section, event-schema.md will have two co-present descriptions of session_log semantics in the same file — the exact drift risk the plan claims to eliminate.
findings.location: AC3 — 'docs/event-schema.md gains a ## The session_log ticket type section AND no longer defers session_log semantics to CLAUDE.md'; docs/event-schema.md line 46
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3's prose: 'The CREATE row's existing inline session_log description should be reduced to a one-line tag plus cross-reference (e.g. "a verbose-log type — see § The session_log ticket type below") so a single authoritative description exists within the file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the What or Design decisions section, spell out the three gate-interaction rules that must be added to concurrency.md I4a: (1) what the parent's plan-review gate check does during a cascade, (2) which ticket types are and are not gate-exempt in the cascade context, and (3) exactly what `--force` propagates to (all ancestors, or only the direct parent).
evidence: Plan What section: 'Fold CLAUDE.md's cascade **gate-interaction** paragraph into `docs/concurrency.md` I4a, which already documents the cascade itself.'
Confirmed absence: grep of docs/concurrency.md for 'gate-exempt', 'plan-review gate', '--force' returns zero hits — the protocol rules are entirely absent from the target file.

plan-review-gate.md line 50: 'Bugs and session_logs are exempt. `--force="<reason>"` bypasses it (audit-logged; on the `transition` path pass `--force` and the `--reason` text becomes the audit note).' — this mentions exemptions but does not define cascade-specific propagation behavior.
The plan names 'epics/stories not gate-exempt' as a specific nuance but provides no definition of this rule anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The plan instructs folding 'CLAUDE.md's cascade gate-interaction paragraph' into docs/concurrency.md I4a, specifying three things that must appear (parent's plan-review gate; epics/stories not gate-exempt; `--force` propagates), but never quotes or specifies the content of that paragraph. The plan's own AC proving command (`rg -n 'gate-exempt|plan-review gate|--force' docs/concurrency.md`) verifies presence of these terms, but the decision rules behind them — which ticket types are gate-exempt in the cascade, under what conditions does `--force` propagate, and what does 'epics/stories not gate-exempt' mean operationally — are not defined in the plan. These are processing protocol details needed by the I4a cascade schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC line 2: '`docs/concurrency.md` I4a gains the cascade gate-interaction nuance (parent's own plan-review gate; epics/stories not gate-exempt; `--force` propagates).'
scenarios: An executor writes the gate-interaction paragraph incorrectly (e.g., states that epics ARE gate-exempt, or that --force does not propagate up the cascade) because the actual rule from CLAUDE.md is not specified.
The AC proof (`rg -n 'gate-exempt|plan-review gate|--force'`) passes with superficially correct term presence but incorrect protocol semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name 'discovered_from' explicitly in the What section's linking bullet (confirming it is a required term in the new doc), or replace the grep proxy with a more reliable check (e.g., verify a dedicated '## Linking' heading exists and has non-trivial content).
evidence: Proving: `rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags' docs/ticket-model.md`
What section: 'linking (the six relations + the promotion concept)' — never names 'discovered_from'
findings.finding: AC1's verify script uses 'discovered_from' as the proxy term for linking coverage (`rg -c 'idea|parent_id|discovered_from|set-tags'`), but the plan body never mentions 'discovered_from' by name — it refers only to 'the six relations + the promotion concept'. If the implementer chooses different relation names or omits 'discovered_from' as a term, the proof step fails even when the content is correct; conversely, a doc that mentions 'discovered_from' once in passing satisfies the grep without substantively covering linking.
scenarios: Implementer writes the linking section using 'blocks/blocked_by, related_to, …' terminology without the exact token 'discovered_from'; AC1 grep returns <4 and fails despite complete coverage.
Implementer adds a single mention of 'discovered_from' in a cross-ref sentence; grep returns 4 but linking is not substantively documented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a pre-work step) that sweeps all docs/ files for 'see CLAUDE.md' or 'CLAUDE.md' cross-references and confirms every such pointer is either fixed in this story or tracked for S2.
evidence: Plan only names one pointer: 'docs/event-schema.md:46 (its CREATE row currently says see CLAUDE.md Session logs)'
No sweep or grep command is specified to find other CLAUDE.md deferrals in docs/
S2 will trim CLAUDE.md content, making any un-fixed deferrals stale
findings.finding: The plan audits and fixes only one known 'see CLAUDE.md' reference (event-schema.md:46) but does not perform or specify a sweep of all docs/ files for similar CLAUDE.md-deferral pointers. If other docs contain 'see CLAUDE.md' for content that is now being homed in docs/, those pointers will be left stale after S2 trims CLAUDE.md.
findings.location: Scope / What section — 'Replace the pointer in docs/event-schema.md:46'
scenarios: A second 'see CLAUDE.md §X' line exists in docs/concurrency.md or docs/user-guide.md; after S2 removes the section from CLAUDE.md, that reference 404s for readers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: `rg -rn 'CLAUDE\.md' docs/ | grep -v 'README'` returns no un-reviewed lines, OR each hit is explicitly noted as to-be-fixed-in-S2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/architecture.md` to scope and update its line-65 `see CLAUDE.md "Session logs"` pointer to reference the new `## The session_log ticket type` section in `docs/event-schema.md` (or add a proving command that `rg -c 'see CLAUDE.md.*Session logs' docs/architecture.md` returns 0 after the work).
evidence: docs/architecture.md:65: 'leave it off so logs stay discoverable (see CLAUDE.md "Session logs")'
Plan What: 'Replace the pointer in docs/event-schema.md:46 … with a real ## The session_log ticket type section' — no mention of architecture.md
Plan Scope In: 'docs/ticket-model.md (new) and docs/{event-schema,concurrency,user-guide,README}.md' — architecture.md is excluded
ABSENCE: no AC item or proving command touches docs/architecture.md
findings.finding: docs/architecture.md:65 also contains a 'see CLAUDE.md "Session logs"' pointer that the plan does not address; after this plan executes, architecture.md will still defer to CLAUDE.md for session_log semantics — leaving a dangling cross-reference if S2 later removes that content from CLAUDE.md. The plan's AC and proving commands are scoped only to the five named files and will not catch this gap.
scenarios: S2 removes the 'Session logs' section from CLAUDE.md; architecture.md:65 now points to a non-existent anchor, silently sending readers to a pruned file.
A contributor reads architecture.md and follows the 'see CLAUDE.md' pointer expecting canonical semantics, but the canonical home is now docs/event-schema.md.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Scope-In list to include `docs/architecture.md` and add a one-line pointer update there; add `rg -c 'see CLAUDE.md.*Session logs' docs/architecture.md` returning 0 to the event-schema.md AC's proving block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'three targeted gap-fills' count in Design decisions with the four existing-file changes enumerated in the What section (add README indexing to the enumerated list, or change the count to four).
evidence: 'this story adds a concept doc + three targeted gap-fills (cascade gate-nuance, a real session_log section replacing an existing CLAUDE.md pointer, and the missing auto-rotation detail)'
What section lists five bullets: ticket-model.md (new), concurrency.md, event-schema.md, user-guide.md, README.md — four existing-file changes
findings.finding: The 'Design decisions' section states 'this story adds a concept doc + three targeted gap-fills', but the 'What' section enumerates four targeted changes to existing docs: (1) concurrency.md gate-interaction, (2) event-schema.md session_log section, (3) user-guide.md auto-rotation, and (4) README.md index entry. An implementer reading 'three' might treat the README index as optional or outside scope.
findings.location: Design decisions paragraph vs. What section (items 1–5)
scenarios: Implementer reads 'three gap-fills', produces the three named ones, skips the README index, and AC5 fails at review.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'three targeted gap-fills' to 'four targeted gap-fills (cascade gate-nuance, a real session_log section, the missing auto-rotation detail, and README index entry)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cross-reference from docs/event-schema.md to docs/ticket-model.md (e.g., a 'See also' line in the session_log section or the file header) so navigability is bidirectional.
evidence: 'Cross-reference docs/event-schema.md for write-level event mechanics' is specified for ticket-model.md only
No reciprocal pointer from event-schema.md → ticket-model.md is mentioned
T7(c) navigability: consumers entering via event-schema.md (a more likely entry point for implementers) will not discover the concept doc
findings.finding: The plan adds a `## The session_log ticket type` section to event-schema.md and creates docs/ticket-model.md, but specifies no reverse navigation link from event-schema.md to ticket-model.md (for readers who encounter event-schema.md first and need the concept doc). Similarly, docs/ticket-model.md will cross-reference event-schema.md, but not vice-versa, leaving the two docs navigable in only one direction.
scenarios: A developer reads event-schema.md to understand event bodies and never discovers that ticket-model.md exists for the concept-level mental model.
findings.suggested_fix: In the new `## The session_log ticket type` section (or the event-schema.md intro), add: 'For the agent-facing concept model, see [docs/ticket-model.md](ticket-model.md).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to the Design decisions section explaining why a new `docs/ticket-model.md` is the right home (e.g., audience separation: agent-facing conceptual reference distinct from the human-facing user-guide and the internals-facing architecture/event-schema docs).
evidence: Plan What: 'Create docs/ticket-model.md holding the agent-facing concept for four topics' — no stated rationale for why a new file vs. extending existing ones
Plan Design decisions: explains WHAT is already homed vs. unhomed, but does not explain WHY a new file is better than extending user-guide.md or architecture.md
ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan explains the audience-targeting or organizational reasoning for docs/ticket-model.md as a new document
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for creating a new `docs/ticket-model.md` file rather than extending existing docs (e.g., user-guide.md for the `idea` status, architecture.md for hierarchy/linking). Per G6, the absence of a positive rationale for the chosen structural approach is a finding.
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer questions why ticket-model.md was created instead of adding the `idea` status section to user-guide.md (where session logs already live) and the hierarchy/linking content to architecture.md.
An implementer misses the 'agent-facing concept doc' intent and places content in the wrong audience section of docs/README.md.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design decisions: 'A dedicated ticket-model.md is chosen over extending user-guide.md (human-facing CLI walkthrough) or architecture.md (internals/contributor focus) because the four topics are agent-facing concepts that span both audiences — they need one coherent reference an LLM agent can read whole without pulling from multiple documents.'

findings.finding: AC3's proving command embeds a `rg -c` invocation inside double quotes that itself contains double quotes, producing a shell-quoting defect: `[ "$(rg -c "see CLAUDE.md .Session logs" docs/event-schema.md)" = 0 ]` — the inner double quotes terminate the outer double-quoted string before `see CLAUDE.md`, causing a syntax error or mis-parsed arguments at execution time.
findings.suggested_fix: rg -q '## The session_log ticket type' docs/event-schema.md && [ "$(rg -c 'see CLAUDE.md.*Session logs' docs/event-schema.md)" = 0 ]

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
